metadata.title: DELAY AND LAG IN FREUD'S THOUGHT: FROM PROJECT FOR A SCIENTIFIC PSYCHOLOGY TO MOSES AND MONOTHEISM

abstract: ) regarded Freud's study on aphasia (1891/1978) as a node between theories developed by linguistics and neuroscience. This article demonstrates that Freud's model of thought can be reformulated using terminologies from modern neurocognitive science and substantiated by findings from qualitative and quantitative analyses. First, this article reinterprets Freud's "Project for a Scientific Psychology" (1895/1950) regarding several theories proposed in cognitive neuroscience. Then, this article analyzes Freud's important works using text mining techniques (especially structural topic model) to reveal how Freud's thought has progressed over time. The force of delay and its effect (latency), for Freud, continued to be essential to the origin of memory until his final essay Moses and Monotheism in 1939(Derrida, 1966/1967). 1 Comparisons of Freud's concepts with neurocognitive scientific terms proved that "Project" can serve as a significant reference point for recent neuroscientific emotion theories.

paragraphs: In 1890s, when Freud was young, he left several drafts in an attempt to scientifically describe memory, e.g., "Project for a Scientific Psychology" (Freud, 1895(Freud, /1950; hereafter referred to as "Project"). For Freud, describing events scientifically meant to ground them in the concepts of physics, under the assumption that all phenomena can be considered to provide important clues to these questions.

paragraphs: The "Project" is based on some concepts of physics, in particular the principle of inertia [das Prinzip der Trägheit]. This principle describes two roles of the nervous system ("Project," p. 296). One receives stimuli from the environment and is excited by them (sensory), while the other discharges its excitement through pathways to the muscular organization (motor), which allows it to remain in a free state for external stimuli. According to Freud ("Project," p. 296), such processes are a primary function of the nervous system.

Neurophysiology was founded on the concepts of irritability or sensitivity to external stimuli by Albrecht von Haller (1708-1777). This understanding of body structure and movement was transmitted to Scottish chemist and physician William Cullen (1710-1790) and physician John Brown (1735-1788), whose work, Elementa Medicinae in 1780 spread throughout Europe. Brown introduced the technical term excitable instead of irritability used by Haller (Porter, 2002, p. 69). In 1790, when Brown's theory was introduced to the German-speaking world, it was widely accepted as a unified biological/neural theory based on a single concept (Huch, 1902, p. 592). Brown's theory influenced German natural philosophy and inspired Romantic physicians to envision a unified view of life. However, these attempts were gradually dismissed as unscientific. In these historical contexts, it is noted that Freud emphasized that his theory of the psyche is based on the principles of natural science.

paragraphs: According to Freud (1895Freud ( /1950)), a main function of the nervous system is memory. Memory is the capacity to be permanently altered by a single event ("Project," p. 299). At the end of the 19th century, the predominant view that there were two different kinds of cells (memory and sensory cells) prevailed, but Freud aimed to repel this idea. In general, when a stimulus is received, there are two types of neurons: those that return to their original state after the wave of movement (excitation) has passed, and those that hold traces ("Project," p. 296). To introduce this distinction in neurons, Freud used the measure of permeability, which divides neurons into two categories: permeable and nonpermeable. As soon as the excitation is over, permeable neurons return to its original state, while non-permeable neurons remain in a different state. This alteration, the difference from its previous state, affords "a possibility of representing memory" ("Project," p. 299). Derrida (1966Derrida ( /1967) called this possibility "as the true origin of memory, and thus of the psyche" (p. 201).

Next, Freud postulated two neuron systems. One was associated with sensory organs and receives external stimuli directly. The other was associated with the internal organization of the body and receives stimuli from the former's nervous system. Freud called the former φ neuron system and the latter as ψ neuron system. From the φ neuron system to the ψ neuron system, through many branches and long pathways, external stimuli are consequently moderated and become more tolerable. In other words, external information is transformed until it reaches the internal nervous system of the body. In both systems, strong stimuli open new pathways where there were no pathways before and spread to greater distances. Through this, excitation is discharged. Freud ("Project," p. 300) called this sequence of phenomena "facilitation [Bahnung]," which means to open up a path [Bahn] in German. Between the two neuron systems, there is a kind of barrier that hinders conduction. Here, resistances arise.

What fundamentally distinguishes Freud's distinction, using permeability as a marker, from the traditional conception of the two types of cells is that permeability is gradational. Non-permeable neurons can become permeable, either gradually or suddenly, when they are repeatedly or strongly stimulated. As a result, external information can be stored in varying degrees in the two neuron systems. When discharge does not occur, excitation accumulates inside the nervous system. Hence, room is created for the development of secondary functions ("Project," p. 296). This process can also explain the lag (delay) between the stimulus (cause) and the expression of responses (symptom). Thus, the key terms that would later become important in Freud's psychoanalytic theory were all lined up in "Project."

When the stimulus is strong, excitation spreads across many pathways. When the same stimulus occurs again, these pathways are activated together again. Between these pathways, an association is thus created. Hence, the following characteristics all confirm Freud's statement that a difference in their essence is replaced by a difference in the environment to which they are destined ("Project," p. 304). First, the only property of neurons, their permeability, can vary depending on the strength of the stimuli they receive. Second, the strength of incoming stimuli depends on the location of the neuron itself (in the φ system or in the ψ system). Lastly, the strength of the stimuli determines the pathways that are activated together.

Next, Freud boldly extended the law of inertia presented in individual neurons to a psychic apparatus composed of neuron systems. If neural excitation flow is dammed, it causes an experience of discomfort. When it is discharged, the discomfort is dispelled, and namely pleasure [Lust] is experienced. Freud called this dynamism, which originates in discomfort and aims at pleasure, the "desire [Wunsch]." Later, in Beyond the Pleasure Principle (Freud, 1920, pp. 37-38), Freud declared that desires are "an expression of the law of inertia in the organism, therefore all desires of the organism are conservative, are acquired historically and tend towards the restoration of an earlier state of things." Already in "Project," when the law of inertia was chosen to explain the fundament of the nervous system, Freud's thought was oriented toward the idea of Thanatos, namely Death drive [Todestrieb]. However, it must be noted that the psychic apparatus, born as a product of stimuli, namely historically acquired, does not know its own state before the stimuli, the pre-historical state to which it should aspire to return.

paragraphs: Freud ("Project," p. 296) himself recognizes, immediately, that the principle of inertia is broken in the nervous system. The nervous system has no defense against stimuli that originate within the organism. The more complex the organization of the organism, the more the ψ-system will accept stimuli from the somatic element itself (i.e., endogenous stimuli), and these will give rise to great needs such as hunger, respiration, and sexuality ("Project," p. 297). It is for these reasons that the organism is compelled to take some action towards the external world to meet these needs, which are known as "the exigencies of life [die Not des Lebens]" ("Project," p. 297). Inside the nervous system, pathway selection is, like avoidance and preference, affected by quantity such as frequency (repetition) and simultaneity. The secondary function identifies these complex differences and selects pathways. In this way, responses to stimuli are recorded as differences between pathways: "Memory is represented by the differences in the facilitations between the ψ neurons" ("Project," p. 300). 3Having described the two nervous systems, Freud asks, "How does quality originate, and where does quality originate?" The memory which is represented by differences between the ψ neurons stays as a reproduction without quality ("Project," p. 308). Quality is only just possible in the third nervous system, ω-neurons. The sensations that arise between ω-neurons should be "conscious sensations" ("Project," p. 308).

A pioneer in the field of emotion research, Antonio Damasio (1994) upended the traditional concept of emotion in the 1990s with his first book on emotion, based on cases of brain-injured patients. He distinguished between emotions that are present in animals, classifying them as Primary Emotions and Secondary Emotions. As an individual grows, the secondary emotional mechanism gradually develops after the primary emotional mechanism. While primary emotions are expressed as instantaneous reactions, secondary emotions are generated through the systematic perception of one's relationship with objects or situations (Damasio, 1994, p. 134). That is, following Damasio's definition, primary emotions are accompanied by quality. As a logical consequence, Freud's primary process, which is reproduction without quality and unknown to consciousness, is prior to Damasio's primary emotions.

The concept of primary and secondary appears again in The Interpretation of Dreams (Freud, 1900), but he introduces it into the psychic process this time. After all, secondary does not imply less complexity or importance, but rather an association with time.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no psychical apparatus which can only perform one primary function, and that such an apparatus is "a theoretical fiction." (Freud, 1900, p. 603) Growth produces secondary processes, and the psychic apparatus arises only after secondary processes have been established (acquired historically). As the logical consequence, the primary process is just a theoretical fiction. Derrida (1966Derrida ( /1967, p. 203) , p. 203) noted that the concept of primary is erased here. As the primary process is a residue of something passed over and prepares for the secondary process, if the primary process is itself a theoretical fiction, the psychic apparatus is based on a secondary process that never knew what passed over at the very beginning, doubly deprived of its origins.

As the logical consequence, the primary process is just a theoretical fiction. Derrida (1966Derrida ( /1967, p. 203) , p. 203) noted that the concept of primary is erased here. As the primary process is a residue of something passed over and prepares for the secondary process, if the primary process is itself a theoretical fiction, the psychic apparatus is based on a secondary process that never knew what passed over at the very beginning, doubly deprived of its origins.
sections.preview_head: Freud ("Project," p. 296) himself recognizes, immediately, that the principle of inertia is broken in the nervous system. The nervous system has no defense against stimuli that originate within the organism. The more complex the organization of the organism, the more the ψ-system will accept stimuli from the somatic element itself (i.e., endogenous stimuli), and these will give rise to great needs such as hunger, respiration, and sexuality ("Project," p. 297).

paragraphs: Indeed, the first simple biological systems did not have any inputs; they did not need them. They simply used an economical motor output, a rhythmic contraction of muscles. (Buzsáki, 2006, p. ix) Buzsáki (2006) assumed that control in living systems begins with the output. The movement of primitive organisms does not occur only after they receive input. Starting from simple primordial forms, organisms evolved by modifying simple rhythmic outputs. Sensation of direction and distance developed only after the invention of movement through space (Buzsáki, 2006, p. ix). In addition, the idea of the 'simple' stimulus in itself is just an abstraction (Buzsáki, 2006, p. 26). Each neuron is not independent but is embedded in a network, and the brain is, working spontaneously, providing information constantly to neurons. These chains cannot be explained by the input/output of a single stimulus and a single neuron. Furthermore, even if the stimuli are the same, the brain is not invariant because of its plasticity. While the brain continues to change, sensory input undergoes step-by-step changes on its way to the brain, so that it becomes even more difficult to interpret neuronal responses (Buzsáki, 2006, p. 30). Thus, to interpret the interaction between the brain and the outer world, it is not effective from the perspective of the external stimuli (Outside-In Strategy). Therefore, Buzsáki proposes to interpret this interaction from the output as a starting point (Inside-Out Strategy). This perspective shift led to focus on endogenous brain activity, which is considered one of the most important discoveries (default mode of brain) in neuroscience in recent decades (Barrett, 2017(Barrett, /2018, p. 58, p. 58).

paragraphs: The Brain Regulates in Advance

Freud's concept of a three-layered nervous system has a pioneering model. British neurologist Hughlings Jackson's (1884Jackson's ( /1927) ) 4 idea of "dissolution (the reverse of the process of Evolution)," one of the great influencers on Freud, described the motor nerve centers in a three-layered structure. According to this theory, the lowest centers are the simplest and most organized. Though they are mostly directly related to a limited region of the body, they are still considered indirect, and therefore are a representation. The middle motor centers are more complex and less organized, and concern wider regions of the body doubly indirectly; hence, they are re-representative. The highest centers which represent widest regions are re-re-representative. Jackson paraphrases the function of being "re-re-representative" as coordinating movements of all parts of the body in the most special and complex combinations (Jackson, 1884(Jackson, /1927, p. 40), p. 40). Buzsáki (2006) noted that the most complex human brain region is a large part of the cortex called the association cortex. It is largely responsible for generating and processing events that unrelated to either sensory input or motor output (Buzsáki, 2006, p. 46). Thus, there is no limit to the number of options that can be associated with the input signal. Hence, it is necessary to reduce the amount of information and options in a complex environment, so that the brain can extract regularities, construct models, and even form biases in advance. In this sequence of processes, past data or experiences will serve as a reference. These perspectives of Buzsáki have an affinity with the theory of predictive coding, in which the brain and body have a top-down as well as a bottom-up relationship and the brain constructs an inner model from past data to generate predictions. They explain the brain's rapid and probabilistically reasonable processing, though, none of them help provide explanations to why this is the case.

paragraphs: Long ago, the world might be once filled with lifeless matter before living beings appeared. The motion of the organism, which initially knew no external input but only output, learned about the external environment for the first time through its own motion. This overlaps with the facilitation described by Freud, in which stimuli open up new paths inside the nervous system. Both are the same events, only what is inside and what is outside is different. Damasio (1999) views consciousness as "a turning point in the long history of life" (p. 4). The condition for the arising of consciousness was to have "boundaries, the separation between what is in and what is out" (Damasio, 1999, p. 135). Every cell body without a nervous system contains mechanisms for minimizing the internal impact of external changes. The internal state of life is extremely sensitive to changes to maintain its existence. Thus, all living beings possess self-regulating systems that work automatically regardless of their will. Thanks to it, they can prevent excessive changes in their internal state and adjust to any changes immediately (Damasio, 1999, pp. 141-142).

With regard to consciousness, Seth (2021) makes a compelling point, namely that "consciousness has more to do with being alive than with being intelligent." Seth believes the essence of one's self is an embodied biological process. A feeling of aliveness is the result of this process, or this dynamism (Seth, 2021, p. 7) and consequently, consciousness is a phenomenon because it is a process. Therefore, Seth stated that it is necessary to distinguish consciousness between its phenomena and its functions (Seth, 2021, p. 14).

paragraphs: In the whole world, all events are continuous in quantity and quality. When a living organism cannot accept the impact from the external environment, it might lead to death. Mechanisms developed within the organism to reduce the impact of their environment, so that the continuous phenomenon is divided into pieces and made acceptable as discontinuous information. In order to ground psychology, a theory of the psyche, on the principles of physics, Freud explained all the phenomena in the nervous systems as quantities. On the other hand, he also tried to explain how an organism perceives quality. Indeed, how can the quantity conveyed within the body be interpreted as something of quality, namely as meaning? Freud thought that quality in its most primal form is expressed as something temporal, and time is expressed as a "period" ("Project," p. 313). Later in "Project," Freud states:

Whereas in the external world the process exhibit a continuum in two directions, according to quantity and period (quality), the stimuli corresponding to them [to the processes] are, as regards quantity, firstly reduced and secondly limited owing to excision, and, as regards quality, are discontinuous, so that certain periods do not operate as stimuli at all. (p. 313)

Here, Freud had shifted the characteristics of the temporal (quality) from periodic to discontinuous, so noted Derrida (1966Derrida ( /1967)). This means that continuous events in the environment are experienced as segmented. In accordance with this, Derrida interpreted that there should be a repetition of alternations between the present (life) and the absent (death) during this process. 5 The idea that an organism is fundamentally embedded in both life and death is supported by neuroscience.

But the problem goes beyond perishability and renewal. Just as death and life cycles reconstruct the organism and its parts according to a plan, the brain reconstructs the sense of self moment by moment. (Damasio, 1999, p. 144) Damasio states that perishability does not inevitably lead to death, nor does death end life. As a result of the cycle of life and death, including the battle between perishability and renewal, the organism is reconstructed on a larger scale. It has no origin prior to the reconstruction itself. Damasio thinks that perishability and renewal are inherent in the sense of self.

Within the greater whole, decay would have been only partial, compensated for by the renewal of another part. When an organism cuts off its environment, it creates a boundary and encloses its interior. Hence, at the same time it was detached from the whole, the decay of the individual could only become the death of itself.

paragraphs: Based on his research on internal receptive senses (interoception), Craig (2015) argued that bodily feelings are the most fundamental biological function. Information about the state of every moment in every part of the body, which Craig calls "an ongoing global emotional moment," is stored in "a quantal storage unit." With each new moment, a quantal storage unit passes to the next, forming a continuous flow of moments that are separated by time intervals. It is the endogenous oscillation that determines this interval, which Craig refers to as "the natural timebase" (pp. 228-230).

This consideration by Craig seems to support the idea of a cycle of life and death, as interruptions are also imprinted in the underlying neural basis of time perception. At the same time, Craig (2015, p. 236) says, the feeling across time. What is continuous through the rupture of the cycle of life and death is feeling. Craig (2015) argued that bodily feelings are the most primal feelings and these feelings make the foundation of the "material self." 6 The endogenous stimuli that Freud considered to be the source of "psychic energy" are renamed as "interoceptive sensations" or Panksepp's "homeostatic affects" (Solms, 2021, p. 48).

Damasio regards what is represented by neural patterns, a coherent collection of patterns of neural activities, as the proto-self, distinguishing it from what we normally perceive as the self. The latter was thereby named the autobiographical self, which is based on our autobiographical memory. Damasio's (1999, p. 154) proto-self is the earliest and simplest manifestations of self and not localized anywhere in the brain, occurring not only in one region in the brain, but in many, at a multiplicity of levels, in brain structures that are interconnected by neural pathways. Concerning the ego, both Craig and Damasio stand on the same basis as Freud. Freud ("Project," p. 323) understood that the ego can be defined as the innervation of the ψ-neuron system in each case. In the history of modern neuroscience, Freud's various proposals can be positioned as a pioneering model. On the other hand, it should be noted that interaction with others (above all, for Freud the Father) is essential for the birth and development of the ego. Freud may have established psychoanalysis because he painfully felt this. Then, it is rather Derrida who will be named as Freud's successor.

Concerning the ego, both Craig and Damasio stand on the same basis as Freud. Freud ("Project," p. 323) understood that the ego can be defined as the innervation of the ψ-neuron system in each case. In the history of modern neuroscience, Freud's various proposals can be positioned as a pioneering model. On the other hand, it should be noted that interaction with others (above all, for Freud the Father) is essential for the birth and development of the ego. Freud may have established psychoanalysis because he painfully felt this. Then, it is rather Derrida who will be named as Freud's successor.

Based on his research on internal receptive senses (interoception), Craig (2015) argued that bodily feelings are the most fundamental biological function. Information about the state of every moment in every part of the body, which Craig calls "an ongoing global emotional moment," is stored in "a quantal storage unit." With each new moment, a quantal storage unit passes to the next, forming a continuous flow of moments that are separated by time intervals. It is the endogenous oscillation that determines this interval, which Craig refers to as "the natural timebase" (pp. 228-230).

This consideration by Craig seems to support the idea of a cycle of life and death, as interruptions are also imprinted in the underlying neural basis of time perception. sections.header_raw: Delay in Many Dimensions and Different Scales
sections.header_norm: delay in many dimensions and different scales
paragraphs: We have many different time scales within the body, ranging from tens of milliseconds to hundreds, to seconds, minutes, and upwards towards even regularities that remain stable over weeks, months, and years (Hohwy, 2013, p. 27). This results in a trillion-fold differences between the smallest and largest time scales. It is generally the case that a trade-off occurs between the time scale and the level of detail. "Fast regularities are processed early in the sensory processing stream [...], and then increasing time scales are processed as the sensory signal works its way up through the primary sensory areas and into higher areas" (Hohwy, 2013, p. 28). The interactions between regularities at different time scales work in two directions: bottom-up and top-down (Hohwy, 2013, p. 31). Therefore, sensory organs that handle faster time-scale regularities work bottom-up, while the top-down operations at higher levels of the hierarchy that process slower timescale regularities are predicted. Ohira (2022) illustrates the various scales within the body as follows (Fig. 1).

Thus, the effects of one system will be "delayed" for other systems and the delay can be tens or trillions of times larger. The body is a stage for spectacular anachronisms and it is extremely difficult to determine when the process began and at what stage it is now. The overwhelming majority remains unconscious, merely as vague physical emotions that tell us whether we are good or bad, pleasant or unpleasant, and prompt us to act. In that sense, too, this nonconscious process is above all an inferential mechanism (Hohwy, 2013, p. 38).

Human perceptions are constructed with a delay. Varied in the process of individual growth, the effects of delays appear on different time scales. Specifically, sexual maturation is the most noticeable of the effects of varied delays. Although most organisms have obvious genital differences at birth, sexual maturity usually occurs much later. On this point, Freud had his own views since very early on. Even though it does indeed seem certain that sexuality and the distinction between the sexes did not exist when life began, it cannot be denied that something that could later be described as "sex" was already included in life at its birth. In fact, with growth of the individual, two opposing forces form the ego, but the belief that they are emerged only after the growth is unsupportable (Freud, 1920, p. 41).
It is as though the life of the organism moved with a vacillating rhythm. One group

Taken from Ohira (2022). The subsystems in our bodies operate on different time scales. The kind of spectacular anachronism that is realized there allows for extremely complex and sophisticated work; however, it is also fragile.

of instincts rushes forward so as to reach the final aim of life as swiftly as possible; but when a particular stage in the advance has been reached, the other group jerks back a certain point to make a fresh start and so prolong the journey. (Freud, 1920, pp. 40-41) In the earliest stages, when excitation and discharge occurred almost simultaneously, life would have been a fleeting phenomenon. Due to the stocking of quantities and delay of discharge, undesirable impacts are retained in the body. This opens up space for the psychic apparatus and prevents instantaneous and unlimited release, as a meandering river cultivates the land. This causes interruptions, which generates a kind of rhythm.

In the view of classical statistical thermodynamics, there is only one kind of interaction between elements-excitation (collision). This can lead to changes in only one direction. However, the brain uses not only excitation but also inhibition in their functions. This additional component is responsible for a fundamental difference between disorder-destined physical systems and order-centered brain dynamics (Buzsáki, 2006, p. 61).

In an excitatory network, excitement can only generate more excitement and move forward, and if the brain network only had an excitatory element, positive forces would drive the system toward only one irreversible end, regardless of the size or form of the system's initial state. However, the brain is a network built from both excitatory and inhibitory elements and can self-organize and generate complex properties (Buzsáki, 2006, p. 63). The interaction between these two elements prevents the brain from falling into a continuous excitatory avalanche or becoming completely suppressed and incapable of responding. Rather, it maintains a type of critical state, called a phase transition in physics. The brain can remain in a metastable state until a perturbation (input from the external world) occurs. Consequently, a system that is maximally sensitive to external perturbations but highly resilient will never fail functionally (Buzsáki, 2006, p. 64).

sections.paper_id: Nakamura - 2023 - DELAY AND LAG IN FREUD’S THOUGHT FROM PROJECT FOR A SCIENTIFIC PSYCHOLOGY TO MOSES AND MONOTHEISM
paragraphs: If the distinction between outside and inside was crucial to life, as Damasio stated, a tremendous sense of loss must have been caused in the individual by cutting off and isolating from the rest of the world. It would have also made the partial decay fatal, which would have been swiftly compensated for by regeneration in the larger whole world. If this is the case, then the decay embedded in individual life would attest to its presence within the whole. The state of the organism's interior, which is a quasi-stable system and called homeostasis, is caused by opposing forces, excitatory and inhibitory. It should be noted that homeostasis is never a static state that is fixed and maintained, but is always regulated upwards (Damasio, 2018, p. 45). When the life-support system consists of the interaction of two opposing forces, and their balance is regulated upwards, the consequence is that the organism can never reach its desired state in which it was assumed to have once been. Thus, while the organism is infinitely deferred from reaching its destination, it is time given to live.

From this perspective, Derrida's remarks about the effect of deferral in Freud's thought are supported. Derrida (1966Derrida ( /1967, p. 199) , p. 199) interpreted that its effects appear in the organism as the vacillating rhythm, as a constant alternation between life and death.

For the organism, stimulus is, after all, nothing but force. In this sense, "the psychic life" is the differences that result from the exercise of various forces, namely the forces of delay (defer; Derrida, 1966Derrida, /1967, p. 201, p. 201). Derrida called Freud as the discoverer of the effect of deferral, but he remarks on Freud's description of the rhythm of hesitation, considering the delay as a result of that struggle, which is more important than the struggle between the two opposing forces. This struggle has been regarded as the fundamental principle explaining all phenomena in physic and physiology-it was Freud's teacher, Brücke, who introduced this principle into physiology-just as the idea of struggle of forces is similar to Darwin's evolutionary hypothesis in the 19th century.
Just as important, Derrida also learned from Freud that the essential differences were replaced by environmental differences and not differences in origin or ethnicity.

The effect of deferral is expressed by a posteriori [Nachträglichkeit] and delaying [Verspätung] and these concepts serve as the basis of all Freud's speculations. Freud extended this discovery to the scale of human cultural history as its ultimate logical consequences in his final essay Moses and Monotheism (Derrida, 1966(Derrida, /1967, p. 203), p. 203).

paragraphs: Moses and Monotheism (Freud, 1939; hereafter referred to as Moses Studies) suggests that there once was a great man named Moses who was murdered. The event was erased, concealed, and distorted in all historical writings. However, the event was not forgotten but grew in significance. Why could such a phenomenon have been considered possible? Freud adopted the assumption of memory latency. Latency is a term derived from pathology, referring to one component of the process of infection and the onset of disease. Nevertheless, it seems impossible to view such a phenomenon not only in the life of a single human being, but in a "race," much less over many generations.

Freud himself goes on to advance this thought, with the proviso that this is just a speculation, and now introduces the idea of "acquired heredity." As Lamarck believed that acquired traits are also inherited, Freud says too that "inherited property" is in each fresh generation, calling not for acquisition but only for awakening (Moses Studies, p.

paragraphs: Although Lamarck's hypothesis of acquired inheritance has long been rejected, recent neuroscience has shown that fears experienced by parents are passed on to the next generation (Debiec & Sullivan, 2014). Furthermore, it also points to the importance of the power of delay. According to Barrett (2020), "the brains of all mammals are built from a single manufacturing plan" (p. 21). To add, genetic evidence suggests that this brain-manufacturing plan holds for reptiles, birds, and some fish. This happens "because the manufacturing process runs in stages, and the stages last for shorter or longer durations in different species" (Barrett, 2020, p. 21). Thus, at least on the level of mammals, Freud's speculation is supported by the fact that their traits manifest themselves differently on different time scales. Moreover, this phenomenon was caused by the forces of delay. Assmann (2010) described Freud's hypothesis as the resonance of memory. The memories that seem to have been forgotten are only latent and continue to resonate and magnify each other (p. 209). Nevertheless, there is one factor that played a decisive role in allowing these phenomena to occur. Freud thought that it must be the "idea of an only, eternal, almighty God" (Moses Studies, p. 110). That is where Freud's own thought process reached, after accumulating speculations over decades.

Furthermore, it also points to the importance of the power of delay. According to Barrett (2020), "the brains of all mammals are built from a single manufacturing plan" (p. 21). To add, genetic evidence suggests that this brain-manufacturing plan holds for reptiles, birds, and some fish. This happens "because the manufacturing process runs in stages, and the stages last for shorter or longer durations in different species" (Barrett, 2020, p. 21). Thus, at least on the level of mammals, Freud's speculation is supported by the fact that their traits manifest themselves differently on different time scales. Moreover, this phenomenon was caused by the forces of delay. Assmann (2010) described Freud's hypothesis as the resonance of memory. The memories that seem to have been forgotten are only latent and continue to resonate and magnify each other (p. 209). Nevertheless, there is one factor that played a decisive role in allowing these phenomena to occur. Freud thought that it must be the "idea of an only, eternal, almighty God" (Moses Studies, p. 110). That is where Freud's own thought process reached, after accumulating speculations over decades.

sections.header_norm: dissonance between wishful state and reality
paragraphs: It can be inferred from this that coincidence between the two cathexes becomes a biological signal for ending the act of thought and for allowing discharge to begin. Their non-coincidence gives the impetus for the activity of thought, which is terminated once more with their coincidence. ("Project," p. 328) When there is a discrepancy between "a wishful state" and reality, we feel uncomfortable and attempt to resolve it consciously or non-consciously. Freud called this action reality probing. Applying this to non-conscious processes in the body, we can say, that the expected state is equivalent to a kind of internal model which the brain either updates its internal model according to incoming information or manipulates sensory signals. When the two come into correspondence with each other and discomfort disappears, this change is experienced as an emotion. Prinz (2004, p. 20) stated that emotions are not merely perceptions of the body but also perceptions of our relation to the world. According to Prinz, mental states represent things because the functions that convey information about them have evolved. In the process of continuously acquiring new representations, our relationship with the world changes. Through adapting to these changes, we utilize acquired representations in new ways, further changing our relations with the world. Printz refers to this function as calibration, which means correction by comparison. This adjusts our behavior towards the external world. For example, coughing is an evolutionarily acquired function for cleaning one's throat. However, coughing may be utilized by spies as a secret communication method. In the first case, a cough is calibrated by a specific cause, while in the second case, a cough is recalibrated by a particular situation. Recalibration represents a relationship with the environment that differs from the original. Prinz (2004, p. 99) referred to the accumulation of past experiences and knowledge used for recalibration as a calibration file.

Although calibration is spontaneous, calibration files can be modified through education and experience. Thus, emotions can be recalibrated and controlled by cultivating a calibration file. However, calibration automatically activates emotions and may lead to bias in judgment. An emotion cannot be altered once it has been triggered. Initiation and reaction pathways of emotions operate without the luxury of control, and reflection and modification of emotions can only be accomplished after sedation (Prinz, 2004, p. 236).

In our children, in adults who are neurotic, as well as in primitive peoples, we meet with the mental phenomenon which we describe as a belief in the 'omnipotence of thoughts.' In our judgment, this lies in an over-estimation of the influence which our mental (in this case, intellectual) acts can exercise in altering the external world. (Moses Studies,p. 113) Belief in the "omnipotence of thoughts" has been supported by language acquisition and development. Language has enabled human beings to construct a simulated reality that is as real as possible. Language became the driving force of the extraordinary advancement in the world of the mind (Moses Studies, p. 113).

The new realm of intellectuality was opened up, in which ideas, memories and inferences became decisive in contrast to the lower psychical activity which had direct perceptions by the sense-organs as its content. This was unquestionably one of the most important stages on the path to hominization. (Moses Studies,p. 113) Any neural pattern can never reach reality. Nevertheless, through language development, intellectuality has gained an advantage over sensory organs that are concerned with the external environment, leading to the opening up of a "new realm of intellectuality" that does not always possess direct perceptual content. Ideas (representations), memories and inferences are decisive and provide a frame of reference for examining and judging sensory signals. In these processes, signals could be manipulated and conformed to internal models or desires. To add, because emotions are, as Prinz stated, perceptions of our relation to the world, reality is nothing more than a representation of the survivor from the struggle between desires and emotions.

Freud's "reality probing" corresponds to the concept of prediction in recent cognitive neuroscience. Barrett (2020) explains prediction as "a conversation with itself." The brain generates predictions based on past data and checks them, comparing with sensory data from the inner body and outer world (Barrett, 2020, p. 74). The chosen prediction is the one that best matches incoming sensory data. What is important to note is that the chosen prediction is not always optimal in a new situation. There are probability issues where even actions based on predictions that are most consistent with sensory data can fail, or the predictions themselves can be wrong. Thus, the brain makes inferences to adjust predictions. That is, when the action is successful, the predictions become established as empirical knowledge, and when the action fails, the model is flexibly updated based on the results of the action (Ohira, 2021). Mach (1883Mach ( /1960, p. 293), p. 293), a contemporary of Freud, questioned if the inconceivable hypotheses of absolute space and absolute time cannot be accepted, in what way can we give a comprehensible meaning to the law of inertia? Freud conceptualized memory in living organisms as an analogy for this law of inertia. Just as there is no absolute space or absolute time, there is also no absolute reference point for the state of an organism. Thus, in Freud's words, an earlier state of things can work as a reference point, and subsequent changes in states are imprinted as memory. This concept of past states and its empirical knowledge as a reference point seems to correspond to the term "prior" from the theory of the Bayesian brain, which also assumes that predictions are generated based on past data stored in the brain (Ohira, 2021). Similar ideas can be found in Damasio (2018, p. 45), that homeostasis is considered to be working for the future of the organism, an inclination to project itself in time by means of optimizing life regulation and possible progeny. As mentioned, homeostasis is maintained by using a past state as the reference point and controlling changes from that point, by always regulating upwards. Thus, insofar as the reference point continues to be "a state of things that has once been attained" (Freud, 1933, p. 106), desired states are transferred from the never before past to the ever-coming future.

Gallese (2022) introduced the words of the Austrian writer Musil, in explaining what time is; an event flows through and ends, that is time, time is human invention. Hence, such a feeling may already contain a delay. Derrida (1966Derrida ( /1967, p. 212) , p. 212) concluded, that deferral is the theme which Freud would have us pursue, and this pursuit is doubtlessly the only one which cannot be exhausted within metaphysics or science.

sections.paper_id: Nakamura - 2023 - DELAY AND LAG IN FREUD’S THOUGHT FROM PROJECT FOR A SCIENTIFIC PSYCHOLOGY TO MOSES AND MONOTHEISM
sections.header_raw: Steps of Freud's Thought Revealed by Statistical Methods
sections.header_norm: steps of freud s thought revealed by statistical methods
paragraphs: It is important to note that the discussion above is based on Freud's earliest drafts. However, Freud's subsequent thoughts and the development of his views from the initial to end point remains unclear. In order to examine this issue, this study utilized the structural topic model (STM) to reconstruct Freud's thought in chronological order. Since many early treatises are short, they were grouped as follows: 1) before On Aphasia in 1891, 2) On Aphasia, 3) between On Aphasia and Studies on Hysteria in 1895, 4) "Project" in 1895, 5) Studies on Hysteria, 6) between "Project" and Interpretation of Dreams in 1900. The Introductory Lectures on Psychoanalysis were further divided into Parts 1 and 2 in 1916 and Part 3 in 1917. Consequently, the target texts were divided into 17 documents according to publication year. The total and different word counts for each document are shown in Table 1.

The topic model is a type of generative model that assumes that each text is generated probabilistically from multiple topics and that each topic is represented by a variety of words with their frequency of occurrence. In this analysis, to focus on the main topics of each period, this article limited the input to common nouns and common verbs. After a prototype summation, words with a frequency of less than 20 were excluded. Additionally, the number of topics was set to three, corresponding to the early, middle, and late periods, respectively.

The results showed that the word "dream [traum]" was prominently and significantly represented in the word cloud that predominated in the mid-term (Fig. 2). This case included a total of 1,925 feature words. Table 2 showed that 75% of the appearances of "dream [traum]" occurred in The Interpretation of Dreams, followed by Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis with less than 20% (Table 2). For relative frequency, the term is also concentrated in these two works (Table 3). Accordingly, it was concluded that "dream [traum]" is almost exclusively contained in The Interpretation of Dreams. After excluding "dream [traum]," which occurred frequently in certain documents, basic verbs (e.g., machen [make] / lassen [let] / geben [give] / kommen [come] / gehen [go]) were found to be the subsequent higher frequency words. The results of STM are shown in Fig. 3 (1,911 feature words). Fig. 4 presents the word clouds of the early predominant, middle and late periods, respectively. Fig. 3 shows that the initially dominant topic gradually recedes and is replaced by the later dominant topic. During the alternation of the two topics, the mid-term topic is dominant. Fig. 4A shows that the topic that characterizes the early period is composed of words such as "syndrome [symptom]," "patient [kranke]," "hysteria [hysterie]," "anxiety neurosis [angstneurose]," "hypnosis [hypnose]," "case [fall]," "memory [erinnerung]," "suggestion [suggestion]," "representation [vorstellung]," which was summarized as "symptom generation" in this study.

Fig. 4B shows that there are more verbs in the middle period than in the other two periods because of various "blundering [fehlleistung]" listed as "case [beispiel]." In addition to verbs, words such as "dream thought [traumgedanke]," "dream content [trauminhalt]," "dream processing [traumarbeit]," "dream interpretation [traumdeutung]," "censorship [zensur]," and "desire fulfillment [wunscherfüllung]" are included, which was summarized as "Dream Formation," as compensation to make up for the blundering behavior.

For the latter period (Fig. 4C), words such as "human being [mensch]," "find [finden]," "god [gott]," "ethnic [volk]," "father [vater]," "child [kind]," "religion [religion]," "group [masse]," "culture [kultur]," "event [vorgang]," and "era [zeit]" form a theme that was summarized as "the Establishment of Religion."

In his Moses Studies, Freud repeatedly stressed that he is not saying anything new, but rather repeating what he had already said 25 years earlier. In fact, in the mid-1910s, when Totem and Taboo was published, the topics of the middle period, "Dream Formation," and the late period, "the Establishment of Religion," switched places, with the latter becoming increasingly dominant thereafter. affinities and similarities of Totem and Taboo and Moses Studies by the co-occurrence networks. In Fig. 5, three main networks of words were formed, with the word "father" at the center of the largest network, followed by "human being" and "god." The network centered on "god" was the smallest, while the network of the word "human being" was disconnected from the other two. The words "father" and "god" are connected through the word "position [stelle]." In Fig. 6, the composition was generally similar, however, the network of "father" and "god" were reversed, with the latter being the largest. There was a strengthening of the connection between "god" and "father" in "position." Notably, in Totem and Taboo, the strongest connection to "father" was "putting away [beseitigung]," whereas in Moses Studies, it was "will [wille]." Similarly, in Totem and Taboo, "god" was associated with "father" and "revere [verehren]" with the same strength, whereas in Moses Studies, "god" was associated with "universal [universal]" and "people [volk]" with the same strength. Words that co-occur with "human being [mensch]" were almost the same in both Totem and Taboo and Moses Studies.

What can be concluded from the above is that Totem and Taboo and Moses Studies seem at first glance to have very different contents. The former speaks exclusively of the Father, while the latter speaks of God. In both, however, one position is spoken of, and in the former it is the Father who occupies that position, while in the latter it is God. In the former, the Father is the being "put away," and in the latter, God is the embodiment of "will."

Thus, the relationship between things that seem unrelated at first glance comes to light by discovering a hidden third term between the two, as Freud's free association method has shown. Yet Freud had already put this method of discovery into practice in "Project" (Fig. 7). It states, "The hidden middle term itself comes never to consciousness.
What is important is to decipher the riddle of the unconscious middle factor [das Mittelglied] itself" ("Project," p. 342).

In this analysis, to focus on the main topics of each period, this article limited the input to common nouns and common verbs. After a prototype summation, words with a frequency of less than 20 were excluded. Additionally, the number of topics was set to three, corresponding to the early, middle, and late periods, respectively.

The results showed that the word "dream [traum]" was prominently and significantly represented in the word cloud that predominated in the mid-term (Fig. 2). This case included a total of 1,925 feature words. Table 2 showed that 75% of the appearances of "dream [traum]" occurred in The Interpretation of Dreams, followed by Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis with less than 20% (Table 2). For relative frequency, the term is also concentrated in these two works (Table 3). Accordingly, it was concluded that "dream [traum]" is almost exclusively contained in The Interpretation of Dreams. After excluding "dream [traum]," which occurred frequently in certain documents, basic verbs (e.g., machen [make] / lassen [let] / geben [give] / kommen [come] / gehen [go]) were found to be the subsequent higher frequency words. The results of STM are shown in Fig. 3 (1,911 feature words). Fig. 4 presents the word clouds of the early predominant, middle and late periods, respectively. Fig. 3 shows that the initially dominant topic gradually recedes and is replaced by the later dominant topic. During the alternation of the two topics, the mid-term topic is dominant. Fig. 4A shows that the topic that characterizes the early period is composed of words such as "syndrome [symptom]," "patient [kranke]," "hysteria [hysterie]," "anxiety neurosis [angstneurose]," "hypnosis [hypnose]," "case [fall]," "memory [erinnerung]," "suggestion [suggestion]," "representation [vorstellung]," which was summarized as "symptom generation" in this study.

Fig. 4B shows that there are more verbs in the middle period than in the other two periods because of various "blundering [fehlleistung]" listed as "case [beispiel]." In addition to verbs, words such as "dream thought [traumgedanke]," "dream content [trauminhalt]," "dream processing [traumarbeit]," "dream interpretation [traumdeutung]," "censorship [zensur]," and "desire fulfillment [wunscherfüllung]" are included, which was summarized as "Dream Formation," as compensation to make up for the blundering behavior.

For the latter period (Fig. 4C), words such as "human being [mensch]," "find [finden]," "god [gott]," "ethnic [volk]," "father [vater]," "child [kind]," "religion [religion]," "group [masse]," "culture [kultur]," "event [vorgang]," and "era [zeit]" form a theme that was summarized as "the Establishment of Religion."

In his Moses Studies, Freud repeatedly stressed that he is not saying anything new, but rather repeating what he had already said 25 years earlier. In fact, in the mid-1910s, when Totem and Taboo was published, the topics of the middle period, "Dream Formation," and the late period, "the Establishment of Religion," switched places, with the latter becoming increasingly dominant thereafter. In Fig. 5, three main networks of words were formed, with the word "father" at the center of the largest network, followed by "human being" and "god." The network centered on "god" was the smallest, while the network of the word "human being" was disconnected from the other two. The words "father" and "god" are connected through the word "position [stelle]." In Fig. 6, the composition was generally similar, however, the network of "father" and "god" were reversed, with the latter being the largest. There was a strengthening of the connection between "god" and "father" in "position." Notably, in Totem and Taboo, the strongest connection to "father" was "putting away [beseitigung]," whereas in Moses Studies, it was "will [wille]." Similarly, in Totem and Taboo, "god" was associated with "father" and "revere [verehren]" with the same strength, whereas in Moses Studies, "god" was associated with "universal [universal]" and "people [volk]" with the same strength. Words that co-occur with "human being [mensch]" were almost the same in both Totem and Taboo and Moses Studies.

What can be concluded from the above is that Totem and Taboo and Moses Studies seem at first glance to have very different contents. The former speaks exclusively of the Father, while the latter speaks of God. In both, however, one position is spoken of, and in the former it is the Father who occupies that position, while in the latter it is God. In the former, the Father is the being "put away," and in the latter, God is the embodiment of "will."

Thus, the relationship between things that seem unrelated at first glance comes to light by discovering a hidden third term between the two, as Freud's free association method has shown. Yet Freud had already put this method of discovery into practice in "Project" (Fig. 7). It states, "The hidden middle term itself comes never to consciousness.

What is important is to decipher the riddle of the unconscious middle factor [das Mittelglied] itself" ("Project," p. 342).

sections.paper_id: Nakamura - 2023 - DELAY AND LAG IN FREUD’S THOUGHT FROM PROJECT FOR A SCIENTIFIC PSYCHOLOGY TO MOSES AND MONOTHEISM
sections.header_raw: Conclusion "Project" as a Reference Point for the Cognitive Neuroscience Emotional Hypothesis
sections.header_norm: conclusion project as a reference point for the cognitive neuroscience emotional hypothesis
paragraphs: Focusing on the concept of the hidden middle factor, we can consider the association between the arguments in "Project" and a theory in recent cognitive neuroscience, as predictive processing (Friston, 2010). Ohira (2021) extended the theoretical framework of predictive processing to higher-order mental functions, related to the generation of meaning of our lives, such as self-esteem, certainty, interpersonal relationship, and symbolic immortality. He argued that predictions are generated by inner models of the aspects of meaning, and prediction errors are calculated by comparing them with representational values of current states of meaning. Regulation is conducted to minimize prediction errors by updating inner models and transforming current states of meaning (Ohira, 2021, p. 25, Fig. 5).

This predictive model of meaning seems to overlap with Freud's concepts of representation and memory, reality examination or censorship, and symptom/dream formation. Namely, representation and memory as calibration file correspond to inner models in predictive processing. It can provide mechanisms for reality examination or censorship for any external and internal information. This process corresponds to the computation of prediction errors. Finally, symptoms or dreams are generated as results of the reduction of prediction errors. These considerations appear to be consistent with the mental functions that Freud intended to express with the term new realm of intellectuality, described above. As the theory of predictive processing posits, the inner model is not necessarily updated, even when prediction errors occur. Prediction errors may even alter reality, as Freud said through the term distracting effect. Immature or fragile mental beings, such as children or neurotic patients, sometimes think what they imagine can be realized, as Freud referred through the term omnipotence of thoughts. However, even healthy adults never grasp objective figures of their external world by passive processing of sensory signals. Instead, we construct the world somewhat subjectively, as predicted by our internal models. From this viewpoint, the new realm of intellectuality exists, where representations, memory, and inferences are predominant over sensory signals conveying figures of the external world. On the other hand, continuous updating of the models by accepting feedback from prediction errors generated by sensory inputs prevents the models from becoming too robust or fragile. If the models are too robust, we will see only what we want to see. However, if the models are too fragile, the world we experience will be stormy and unstable. These mechanisms ensure that the new realm of intellectuality is not dogmatic.
